Correlation layout: wide

> |









TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





jdesc() is the counterpart of SPSS’s Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Descriptives – the same N, minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation you’d read from that output table. And the check above may feel familiar from practice: reopening a dataset that’s been through another format, and confirming the descriptives still match.
The matching numbers are only half the story – the missing-value declarations made the round trip too, which is arguably the bigger deal. You saw it back in block 10: Education’s codes come through meaning the same thing in every file, with the Stata copy simply wearing its .a/.b form. In SPSS terms, your MISSING VALUES definitions survived the Save As. (Age itself carries no missing codes – that’s exactly why it makes a clean test variable here.)









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





For a clean variable like Age, base R agrees exactly: mean(MyData_r$Age) returns the same 40.65049. The difference appears with variables that carry declared missing-value codes, like Income – those codes are ordinary numbers to base R, and the block’s last lines show the damage live: mean(MyData_spss$Income) folds the -99s and -98s straight into the average, while jdesc() reads the declarations and leaves the codes out automatically – provided the declarations exist: here they traveled with the files, and when a dataset arrives with codes that were never declared, you add the declarations yourself with jstats’s jdeclare_udm() function. It’s the same protection you’d expect from commercial statistical software.









NoteWhat’s the point?





The download at the bottom of this page is the finished version of what you built – and finished means something specific. A goal every experienced programmer and statistician takes for granted, but new researchers are rarely told: a completed script should run from beginning to end without errors. On this page you experimented – deliberate errors and prompts included, because that’s how working scripts develop. The download shows what the end of that process looks like, and it differs from the page blocks in three principled ways: the deliberate error lines are commented out, the y/n prompt line is commented out (a prompt would pause an unattended run), and the saves – plus the one reload that could collide with an earlier run’s leftovers – carry overwrite = TRUE so the whole file can be run again and again, exactly the automatic-execution override from block 6.
To prove a script is finished, use the Source icon (to the right of Run in the script toolbar): it runs the entire file, top to bottom, in one go. A few things to know. Source shows the output without echoing each command back to the Console – that’s normal – and the small arrow beside Source offers “Source with Echo” if you’d like the commands shown too. jstats functions print their own tables, so their output always appears; but a bare base R line that merely displays a value – ls(), getwd(), mean() – stays quiet under Source, because R only auto-prints results when you run lines interactively – and even interactively, what it prints is a bare, unlabeled number. That’s why the script prints its base R mean with cat(), a label, and a few blank lines to set it apart – hand-formatting that jstats tables do for you. And if you re-run the script you pasted together yourself, the saves will refuse to overwrite their earlier files – that’s block 6’s lesson again; add overwrite = TRUE just as the finished file does.
The What you should see boxes under some blocks above reproduce that Console – the blue command lines, the gray notices, the colored tables, a red error where one is meant to happen. They show the code being run, along with any note written on the end of a command line – but not the standalone ## comment lines (running one just echoes it back and does nothing), so the box stays focused on what actually happens. Reading a block? The commented code is in the gray block; the result is in the box beneath it. Several boxes also picture your Environment pane – the upper-right one – so you can watch objects appear and disappear as you work. And where a file path appears, the boxes print ~/your-working-directory/ in place of a real folder name: on your own screen that reads as the actual path to your project.
Nothing needs deleting between runs, and nothing is deleted: the overwrite arguments handle the repeats, and the Data folder with its three saved files stays put after every run – that’s what saving is for. Remove the files by hand if you ever want a clean slate.
One more setup idea if you’re keeping the file – and it’s worth keeping: finished scripts travel. Sooner or later you’ll share one with a supervisor or collaborator, or receive one from someone else, and a script that runs top to bottom on another person’s computer is the standard to aim for. So a downloaded script deserves a proper home: jstats created the Data folder for you during the run-through, but you can also make folders yourself in RStudio – in the Files tab of the lower-right pane, click New Folder and type a name into the popup. “Scripts” is a good one for storing this download, and it will appear right beside the Data folder you created by following the steps above.

title: jstats
text:
This page is a hands-on run-through of moving data in and out of jstats: loading files, saving your work back out, and converting between formats.






NoteBefore you start





To follow along you’ll need:

R, RStudio, and jstats installed. If they’re not, work through Get Started, Steps 1-5 first and come back.
An RStudio Project open, so your working directory and files are already set up.
A new, empty R script open (File > New File > R Script). You’ll copy each numbered block into it, one at a time, running each before moving on.










NoteHow to Use this Page





The blocks on this page build one script. Paste each block into the same R script file as you reach it, in order — by the end, the whole file runs cleanly from top to bottom, except in the few places where a comment says an error is deliberate (so you can see what one looks like).
A few mechanics worth knowing:

Every block starts with a dashed comment header (## ---- 1. ... ----). It marks where one block ends and the next begins in your growing script — and RStudio reads those headers as sections, so they appear in the script’s outline for easy jumping around.
Experiment freely as you go — change a number, rerun a line, poke at things. Building the script yourself is the point.
We recommend working through the blocks one at a time first – you learn far more by building the script yourself and watching each piece run than by reading a finished file. Once you’ve done that (or if you’d rather grab everything at once), the complete, clean script – without your experiments, and finished so it runs top to bottom – is available to download at the bottom of the page.

This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group, so once you’ve read it here, you can skip it elsewhere.









TipNew to R?





You don’t need to memorize any of this yet. Right now the goal is simply to run each line and watch what happens – the ideas settle in as you go. A few things R doesn’t care about, so you don’t have to either: which line numbers your code lands on, and how many blank lines sit between commands – paste each block anywhere below your previous code and it all works the same. Lines starting with # are comments: notes for humans that R skips entirely (extra hash marks, like ##, are still just a comment). And running a line a second time is almost always harmless – you’ll simply get the same result again. If a line doesn’t work, check that jstats loaded without errors, then try it again.









NoteReset your session





Experimenting means the occasional wrong turn – a setting changed, the practice dataset overwritten, output that suddenly looks different. When something feels off and you’d like a clean footing without restarting RStudio, run these two lines in the Console:
## Put jstats' output settings back to their defaults
joutput("standard")   # output level, and decimal places back to 3

## Point saving and loading back at your working directory
joptions(data.dir = "")
That resets jstats itself. To clear away objects you created while following along – data frames like MyData_r, or a value like MyObject – the simplest way is the little broom icon at the top of the Environment pane (upper-right): one click sweeps the pane clean. It clears everything in the Environment, so use it when you’re between run-throughs, not in the middle of one. (You can also remove a single object by name, e.g. rm(MyData_r).) The built-in practice data comes right back with jload("community") whenever you want it.
This box is the same on every hands-on page in this group.





We’ll start by loading the package and one of its built-in practice datasets.
## ---- 1. Load the package and a dataset ---------------------------------

## Load the jstats package (needed once per R session)
library(jstats)

## Load the package dataset called community
## It's loaded as a data frame into the Environment
jload("community")






NoteWhat you should see






The command lines are shown; the standalone ## comment lines aren’t – running a comment just echoes it back and does nothing.


Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminal×Background Jobs×> library(jstats)
jstats v0.9.103 is up to date.
> jload("community")
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'community'.
5 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Income: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Education: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Smoker: -99 ["Refused"]
  Environment1: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Environment3: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(community, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(community, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


community103 obs. of 15 variables












NoteWhat’s the point?





library(jstats) switches the package on for the current session – and once per session is all it takes. If you’ve just come from the Quick Start page, jstats may well be loaded already; running the line again is harmless (it simply does nothing the second time). You can check at any point in RStudio’s Packages tab (lower-right pane): the checkbox beside jstats is ticked while the package is loaded.

On a fresh load, jstats also prints a short version message in the Console; if it was already loaded, the line stays quiet. And if you’d rather never type the line again, R can run it for you at the start of every session – the Loading jstats automatically page shows how.









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





For a dataset that ships inside a package, base R has one too: data(community) loads it. The difference shows up beyond package data: data() only reaches datasets bundled in packages, while jload() is one loader for everything on this page – package datasets, your saved .rds files, and SPSS, Stata, SAS, Excel, and CSV files – with the same one-line call.






Anything you create in R is stored as an object, and objects live in the Global Environment – the workspace RStudio shows you in the Environment tab of the upper-right pane. Here we make two objects of our own, and open a second dataset alongside the first.
## ---- 2. Objects, the assignment operator, and the Global Environment ----

## "Objects" other than data frames can go into the Environment
## The "Assignment Operator" (less-than followed by a dash) is a key Base R fundamental
## Take the thing on the right and assign it to what's on the left
## Take the Region variable from the community data frame and assign it to a new object
## The new object we'll call "MyObject"
MyObject <- community$Region ## (Base R code)

## The name on the left is entirely yours to choose, and the thing on the
## right can be anything at all -- here, just the number 5
x <- 5

## You are not limited to one dataset at a time
## Load a second package dataset, clinic, alongside community
jload("clinic")






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminal×Background Jobs×> MyObject <- community$Region ## (Base R code)
> x <- 5
> jload("clinic")

Suspected missing-value codes detected:
  MoodRating:  -99 (4), -98 (3)
  Anxiety2:    -99 (3), -98 (3)

These codes are not formally defined, so they are not treated as missing.
Declare them as missing if they are; leave as-is if real.

# To declare one variable's codes as missing:
  clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating, codes = c(-99, -98))
# To label them at the same time (example only):
#   clinic <- jdeclare_udm(clinic, MoodRating, codes = c("label1" = -99, "label2" = -98))
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'clinic'.
4 variables have user-defined missing values:
  Stress: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  SleepHours: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
  Medication: -99 ["Refused"]
  Anxiety4: -99 ["Refused"], -98 ["Don't know"]
These codes are excluded as missing in jstats analyses. For better base R compatibility, convert them:
  jconvert(clinic, to = "stata")  - retains missing-value codes, base R compatible (recommended)
  jconvert(clinic, to = "baseR")  - converts to plain NA and removes missing-value codes
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


clinic70 obs. of 16 variables


community103 obs. of 15 variables


Values


MyObjectinteger+lbl [1:103] 1, 3, 1, 1, 1, 2, 1, 4, 1, 2…


x5












TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





The Global Environment has no real SPSS counterpart, and that is the most useful thing to know about it. In SPSS everything lives inside a dataset: COMPUTE x = 5. does not create an object called x – it adds a column of 5s, one value per case, to whatever dataset is active. There is nowhere for a single value, or a single variable, to sit on its own. (SPSS macros come closest, and hardly anyone uses them.) In R the Environment is exactly that place: a workspace holding objects of any shape, and a data frame is simply one of the shapes.
SPSS can have several datasets open too – but each opens in its own Data Editor window, and switching the active one takes a syntax chain most users never bother with:
GET FILE='community.sav'.
DATASET NAME community.
GET FILE='clinic.sav'.
DATASET NAME clinic.
DATASET ACTIVATE community.
In R there are no windows to juggle: the Environment holds several data frames side by side, and every command simply names the one it uses – the $ in community$Region means “the Region column, in the community dataset.”






Objects you no longer need can be removed, and there are two ways to do it. rm() takes out exactly what you name and leaves everything else alone – which is what you want when one object is wrong and the others are fine. The broom in the Environment pane’s toolbar takes out everything, in one click; RStudio asks you to confirm before it does. The code below uses rm(); the red arrow in the box points out the broom.
One thing to expect: rm() says nothing at all when it works. Silence is success – the Environment pane is where you check.
## ---- 3. Cleaning up ----------------------------------------------------

## Remove MyObject from the Environment
## Note that this is a Base R function (j is not the first letter)
rm(MyObject)

## Remove x as well
rm(x)

## Remove the community data frame
rm(community)

## Remove the clinic data frame
rm(clinic)






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminal×Background Jobs×> rm(MyObject)
> rm(x)
> rm(community)
> rm(clinic)
> |

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Environment is empty




The red arrow points to the broom – RStudio calls it Clear objects from the workspace. It empties the Global Environment in one go, which is the button form of the rm(list = ls()) line you will meet later on this page.










TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





When you have finished with a dataset in SPSS you close its Data Editor window, or run DATASET CLOSE clinic. – rm() is the R counterpart. The difference is that the Environment holds more than datasets, so rm() removes any object you name, whatever its shape.






jstats can show more or less detail. The output level controls how much appears – so with the Environment empty, load community again and watch it say less.
## ---- 4. How much output you see ----------------------------------------

## The output level is set to "standard" by default
## After you learn more about jstats (and R) you might not want all of that output
joutput("minimal")

## With minimal output, you get less information
jload("community")

## Set the output level back to standard
joutput("standard")






NoteWhat you should see






Console (lower-left pane)

ConsoleTerminal×Background Jobs×> joutput("minimal")

  regression.ci: OFF
  means.ci: OFF

> jload("community")
Loaded the jstats example dataset 'community'.
> joutput("standard")

  levene: OFF

Environment (upper-right pane)


EnvironmentHistoryConnectionsTutorial


Data


community103 obs. of 15 variables












NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





There isn’t one. Base R has no output-level setting – each function prints whatever it prints, and how much you see varies with each package author’s choices. joutput() is a single, jstats-wide control.






Most functions need something inside the parentheses, but a few work on their own. Every function also has a help page.
## ---- 5. Functions with no arguments, and getting help ------------------

## A few functions work correctly without input between the parentheses, but most require input
jload()  # This will cause an error with a message about how you might fix the error

## But this one will show you the current output settings for jstats
joutput()

## Here's a Base R function that works without arguments/input
## List the objects/data frames in the Environment
ls()

## Here's another one from Base R
## A working directory is the "path" to where your project is stored on your computer
## If you're not working in a project, you have to set this yourself (finicky)
getwd()

## The technical name for a function's inputs are "Arguments"
## View help files describing necessary and optional Arguments by running...
?jload

## The same ? help works for base R functions and functions from any installed package
?ls

## And this one opens the package overview: every jstats function, grouped by purpose
?jstats






NoteAside: how R writes file paths





A path is a folder’s address on your computer – the folders you would click through, written out on one line. Three spelling quirks are worth thirty seconds each, because you will meet all three on this page.
The ~ shorthand. Look at the top of your Console: RStudio shows your working directory with a squiggle, something like ~/your-working-directory/. The ~ is shorthand for your home folder (on Windows, usually your Documents folder). getwd() prints that same address written out in full – beginning, on Windows, with a drive letter such as C: or E: that names the disk. Short spelling in the Console header, long spelling from getwd(). Same place.
You have met that squiggle before, on the Quick Start page, doing a completely different job – writing a model, as in jt(Optimism ~ Gender). In a file path it has nothing to do with models. Same character, two unrelated jobs.
The slashes. R writes paths with forward slashes (/) on every kind of computer. Windows itself writes the same address with backslashes: File Explorer shows E:\00 R Projects, R shows E:/00 R Projects. Same folder, two spellings. When you type a path in R, use /.
So: ~ means home, / goes between folders, and getwd() tells you the full address of where you are working.
And if all of that felt like a lot, don’t worry. You will not have to type a path anywhere on this page. As long as you work inside an RStudio Project – which is what Before you start asks of you – jstats handles the addresses for you. In a moment you will name a folder once, with joptions(data.dir = "Data"), and jstats creates it inside your project; from then on you load and save with plain file names like "MyData_r.rds", and jstats works out where they belong. Paths are worth recognizing when a message prints one. They are not something you need to write.









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





? is base R – ?jload, ?ls, and ?mean all work the same way, for base functions and for functions from any installed package. (In RStudio, putting the cursor on a function name and pressing F1 does the same thing. On Macs the key varies from machine to machine – F1 on some, fn + F1 on others, and on some Macs the function keys must first be enabled in System Settings.) What a base R setup doesn’t have is a grouped index: a typical workflow spans functions scattered across many packages, each with its own alphabetical help listing. ?jstats shows the whole toolkit on one page, organized by purpose – one advantage of the functions traveling together as a package.






When you load a dataset that’s already open, jstats checks with you before overwriting it – watch for the y/n prompt in the Console window.
## ---- 6. The overwrite prompt -------------------------------------------

## The output you get in the Console Pane can include a confirmation prompt
## Here, jstats will not overwrite community unless you give it permission
jload("community") # Look at the y/n prompt in the console

## Set an optional overwrite argument/input to overwrite without the prompt
jload("community", overwrite = TRUE)






NoteWhat’s the point?





The overwrite argument is here to show you two things:

What an argument looks like. Options go inside the parentheses as name = value – here, overwrite = TRUE.
Prompts can be overridden. The few times jstats asks for confirmation, the prompt appears in the Console – and each one can be switched off with the right argument. That matters when code runs automatically, with nobody at the keyboard to answer: it happens in several places behind this website, and in the finished script you can download at the bottom of this page.










NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





Base R doesn’t ask. Loading or assigning over an existing object replaces it silently – no prompt, no warning, and the old copy is gone. The confirmation prompt is a jstats safeguard.






Let’s save your work – first with no folder specified, to see where files go by default, and then to a Data folder of your own.
## ---- 7. Saving to a Data folder ----------------------------------------

## Use the assignment operator to copy community to a new data frame called MyData
MyData <- community

## Save it with no folder specified -- where does the file go?
## The console message shows the full path: it landed in your project's
## working directory, the default location
## It's the same path the Base R getwd() command showed you earlier
jsave(MyData, "WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Delete that test file
## file.remove() is base R -- the file-on-disk equivalent of the rm()
## you used for data frames. It replies with a cryptic [1] TRUE
file.remove("WhereDidIGo.rds")

## Now name a Data folder of your own
## jstats will create this folder upon first save
## Or you can create it manually
joptions(data.dir = "Data")

## Save the dataset to the Data folder and call it MyData_r.rds
## Specify R native format with the .rds extension
## The Data folder is created now, on this first save
jsave(MyData, "MyData_r.rds")

NoteWhat’s the point?





Why set a data folder at all? Tidiness now, and a payoff later. With data.dir set, every save and load on this page knows where to look. And that’s the habit to keep: put joptions(data.dir = "Data") near the top of your own scripts, then drop a dataset of yours into that folder and jload("YourFile.sav") opens it by name alone. If you’re not sure where the folder is on your computer, the jsave message has already told you – it prints the full path each time, ready to paste into File Explorer (Windows) or find in Finder (Mac).









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





Saving to the working directory by default is base R’s behavior too – saveRDS(MyData, "MyData_r.rds") puts the file in the same place; jstats just tells you, in full, where it went. To use a folder, base R won’t create it for you (that’s dir.create("Data") first), and the folder name gets typed into every path from then on – with any subfolders along for the ride, e.g. saveRDS(MyData, "Data/CommunityStudy/Wave1/MyData_r.rds"). joptions(data.dir = ...) sets the location once, and every jsave() and jload() after that already knows where to look.
And note what file.remove() didn’t do: ask. Like the silent overwrite in block 6, base R deletes with no confirmation – mix up a filename and a real data file is gone. jstats’s prompts exist precisely because of edges like that.

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





In SPSS, user-missing values are declared in Variable View and travel with the file. When you Save As a different format, those definitions don’t always carry across cleanly. jstats keeps the equivalent information with your data, and when a format needs it in a different form, jconvert() does the translation – which is why saving straight to Stata format above stops and asks you to convert first.









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





Base R alone can’t write SPSS or Stata files. You’d install and load the haven package and call haven::write_sav() or haven::write_dta() yourself – and haven is exactly the proven package jstats uses under the hood, so nothing here reinvents the wheel. What jstats spares you is the setup and the sharp edge: loading an extra package in every script, learning its separate function set, and handling the missing-value translation by hand – haven refuses SPSS-style missing codes headed into Stata format, just as you saw above, and converting them yourself is genuinely fiddly. jconvert() is that translation, done for you.
Two conversion habits are worth knowing even if you’re an experienced R user.

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





SPSS output shows a fixed number of decimals, padding with zeros to fill the width – a mean of 40.65 appears as 40.650 in a three-decimal table. jstats goes the other way: it never prints trailing zeros, so digits sets a maximum, and each column shows only the decimals it actually needs. That’s why the mean above reads 40.65 at the default setting of 3 – its third decimal happens to be a zero. And if a statistic lands exactly on a round value, it displays short even at digits = 7: that’s trimming, not truncation or a stuck setting.









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





The closest is options(digits = ...), and it’s a different animal: it sets significant digits, globally, for nearly everything R prints. joutput(digits = ...) means decimal places, and it changes only jstats output.






Those three data frames each made a round trip through a different file format. If the trips were truly lossless, the same statistics should come back from every one of them – right down to the seventh decimal.

TipComing from SPSS, Stata, or SAS?





jdesc() is the counterpart of SPSS’s Analyze > Descriptive Statistics > Descriptives – the same N, minimum, maximum, mean, and standard deviation you’d read from that output table. And the check above may feel familiar from practice: reopening a dataset that’s been through another format, and confirming the descriptives still match.
The matching numbers are only half the story – the missing-value declarations made the round trip too, which is arguably the bigger deal. You saw it back in block 10: Education’s codes come through meaning the same thing in every file, with the Stata copy simply wearing its .a/.b form. In SPSS terms, your MISSING VALUES definitions survived the Save As. (Age itself carries no missing codes – that’s exactly why it makes a clean test variable here.)









NoteWhat’s the Base R equivalent?





For a clean variable like Age, base R agrees exactly: mean(MyData_r$Age) returns the same 40.65049. The difference appears with variables that carry declared missing-value codes, like Income – those codes are ordinary numbers to base R, and the block’s last lines show the damage live: mean(MyData_spss$Income) folds the -99s and -98s straight into the average, while jdesc() reads the declarations and leaves the codes out automatically – provided the declarations exist: here they traveled with the files, and when a dataset arrives with codes that were never declared, you add the declarations yourself with jstats’s jdeclare_udm() function. It’s the same protection you’d expect from commercial statistical software.









NoteWhat’s the point?





The download at the bottom of this page is the finished version of what you built – and finished means something specific. A goal every experienced programmer and statistician takes for granted, but new researchers are rarely told: a completed script should run from beginning to end without errors. On this page you experimented – deliberate errors and prompts included, because that’s how working scripts develop. The download shows what the end of that process looks like, and it differs from the page blocks in three principled ways: the deliberate error lines are commented out, the y/n prompt line is commented out (a prompt would pause an unattended run), and the saves – plus the one reload that could collide with an earlier run’s leftovers – carry overwrite = TRUE so the whole file can be run again and again, exactly the automatic-execution override from block 6.
To prove a script is finished, use the Source icon (to the right of Run in the script toolbar): it runs the entire file, top to bottom, in one go. A few things to know. Source shows the output without echoing each command back to the Console – that’s normal – and the small arrow beside Source offers “Source with Echo” if you’d like the commands shown too. jstats functions print their own tables, so their output always appears; but a bare base R line that merely displays a value – ls(), getwd(), mean() – stays quiet under Source, because R only auto-prints results when you run lines interactively – and even interactively, what it prints is a bare, unlabeled number. That’s why the script prints its base R mean with cat(), a label, and a few blank lines to set it apart – hand-formatting that jstats tables do for you. And if you re-run the script you pasted together yourself, the saves will refuse to overwrite their earlier files – that’s block 6’s lesson again; add overwrite = TRUE just as the finished file does.
The What you should see boxes under some blocks above reproduce that Console – the blue command lines, the gray notices, the colored tables, a red error where one is meant to happen. They show the code being run, along with any note written on the end of a command line – but not the standalone ## comment lines (running one just echoes it back and does nothing), so the box stays focused on what actually happens. Reading a block? The commented code is in the gray block; the result is in the box beneath it. Several boxes also picture your Environment pane – the upper-right one – so you can watch objects appear and disappear as you work. And where a file path appears, the boxes print ~/your-working-directory/ in place of a real folder name: on your own screen that reads as the actual path to your project.
Nothing needs deleting between runs, and nothing is deleted: the overwrite arguments handle the repeats, and the Data folder with its three saved files stays put after every run – that’s what saving is for. Remove the files by hand if you ever want a clean slate.
One more setup idea if you’re keeping the file – and it’s worth keeping: finished scripts travel. Sooner or later you’ll share one with a supervisor or collaborator, or receive one from someone else, and a script that runs top to bottom on another person’s computer is the standard to aim for. So a downloaded script deserves a proper home: jstats created the Data folder for you during the run-through, but you can also make folders yourself in RStudio – in the Files tab of the lower-right pane, click New Folder and type a name into the popup. “Scripts” is a good one for storing this download, and it will appear right beside the Data folder you created by following the steps above.

text:
This page gets the jstats package installed and loaded, ready to use. It assumes you’ve already installed R and RStudio and have an RStudio Project open. If you haven’t, work through Install R and RStudio and RStudio Orientation first, then come back here.






TipFor experienced R users





If you’ve installed R packages before, this is ordinary install-and-load with one small wrinkle. jstats isn’t on CRAN yet, so it installs from an r-universe repository (a free, CRAN-like service that publishes ready-made package binaries). In practice that means a single install.packages() call with a repos argument — the r-universe repository for jstats itself, plus a regular CRAN mirror for any supporting packages — shown just below. Because you’re installing a binary, nothing is compiled, so there’s no need for Rtools (Windows) or Apple’s command line tools (Mac), and no remotes or GitHub step. jstats also checks for a newer version each time it loads, and jupdate() updates it in a single command. Jump to the install line below — the rest is the install-and-load you already know.

text:

TipFor experienced R users





If you’ve installed R packages before, this is ordinary install-and-load with one small wrinkle. jstats isn’t on CRAN yet, so it installs from an r-universe repository (a free, CRAN-like service that publishes ready-made package binaries). Jump to the install line below — the rest is the install-and-load you already know.

title: jstats
text:
What you need first
Before the steps below, make sure you have:

R and RStudio installed — see Install R and RStudio.
An RStudio Project open — RStudio Orientation covers creating one.

title: jstats
text:
Install jstats and load it
jstats installs with a single command. Click in the Console (bottom-left pane), paste the two lines below, and press Enter — the first installs jstats, the second loads it:
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)
You may see a red WARNING that Rtools is required. That’s normal and not an error — jstats installs without it. And if R asks whether you want to install from sources, click No.
The repos part just tells R where to look: the first address is the r-universe repository where jstats is published, and the second is an ordinary CRAN mirror that supplies any of jstats’s supporting packages you don’t already have. Both hand over ready-made versions, so nothing is built from source and the install is quick.






TipNew to R?





Two words are about to start doing a lot of work, and they’re easy to mix up.
A package is the thing you install — jstats is a package. A library is a folder on your computer where your installed packages are kept. install.packages() puts jstats into the library; library(jstats) fetches it back out for the session. The command is named after the folder it fetches from.
That’s also why R can answer a line that says library with an error that says package: it looked in the folder, and the package wasn’t there. And it’s why updating R means installing your packages again — a new version of R starts with a fresh, empty library.



Installing is normally a once-per-computer job — plus once more if you ever update R itself, which comes out in a new version about once a year and starts a fresh package library when it does. Worth bookmarking: this page always carries the current install line, so it’s the one to come back to on a new computer or after an R update. (If library(jstats) ever fails after updating R, the Troubleshooting page has the details.)
On the first install — or the first time you load jstats after updating R — you may see a line like package 'jstats' was built under R version 4.x.x. That’s normal and needs no action: it only means the ready-made copy was built on a slightly different R version than the one you’re running. It clears the next time you update R.
If it loaded cleanly, you’ll see a short jstats startup message — and, if a newer version is already out, a note with the exact lines to update (more on that below). That message is how you know jstats is loaded and ready.
You’ll run library(jstats) at the start of each session. Prefer not to? You can set your Project to load it for you automatically — see Loading jstats automatically.

If the install fails
The most common snags are below — scan the titles and open the one that matches your error. If yours isn’t here, the full Troubleshooting page has more.






WarningThe install seems half-done or broken





The simplest fix is to remove jstats and install it fresh:
remove.packages("jstats")
install.packages("jstats",
  repos = c("https://jma61.r-universe.dev", "https://cloud.r-project.org"))
library(jstats)









WarningRStudio hangs or loops every time it starts





This almost always means an install command was put in your .Rprofile. That file runs every time R starts, so an install line tries to reinstall on every startup and can loop. Your .Rprofile should only ever hold lightweight startup lines like library(jstats) — never an install command. To recover: close RStudio, open your Project folder in your computer’s file manager, open the .Rprofile file in a plain text editor (Notepad on Windows, TextEdit on Mac), delete the install line, keeping the library(jstats) line (and its reminder comment, if your .Rprofile has one), save, and reopen RStudio.

title: jstats
text:
Keep jstats up to date
Because jstats is updated often during this phase, it checks for a newer version each time you load it with library(jstats). When one is available, it prints a short message at startup — the new version number and the one command to run. That command is:
jupdate()
Run it in the Console and jstats updates itself: one command, with nothing to copy or keep track of. jupdate() first checks that you’re online and that you aren’t already on the latest version (if you are, it just says so and stops), then downloads and installs the new version. It doesn’t pause to ask “are you sure?” — it goes ahead and updates.
One thing to know: installing the new version and loading it are two separate steps. jupdate() puts the new version on your computer, but the session you’re in keeps running the old one until you restart R. You don’t have to remember how — when the update finishes, jupdate() prints the exact steps:
jstats has been updated.

Restart R to load it in RStudio:
  - open the Session menu > choose Restart R
  - or press Ctrl+Shift+F10

The Console will return to a blank prompt.
Reload jstats with library(jstats) (unless loaded automatically on startup).
Follow those and you’re running the new version.
(This all gets simpler once jstats reaches CRAN: jupdate() keeps working, just updating the ordinary way, and we’ll revise these instructions then.)






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





A couple of extra options. If you’d rather confirm before anything installs, run jupdate(ask = TRUE) — it shows the available and installed versions and waits for your okay. Updates are reliable here because jupdate() installs in a separate R process, so the copy loaded in your session never has to overwrite its own files mid-update (the usual cause of a failed update on Windows). And to silence the startup check entirely — for instance on a machine that’s on a network but has no internet — set options(jstats.check_updates = FALSE).









ImportantThis is a beta, and your input shapes it



While jstats is in this pre-release phase, it’s still actively growing — which makes now the best time to ask for things. If a function is missing an option your work needs, or you’d like an analysis it doesn’t yet cover, say so by email: SurveyCentre@griffith.edu.au. If you hit an error, include the full error message, what you ran, and which versions of R and jstats you’re on (you can get those by running R.version.string and packageVersion("jstats")) — that detail makes a fix far quicker. Changes are far easier to fold in now than after the package reaches its stable release, where keeping existing code working limits what can change.








TipFor experienced R users





A concrete example of feature-by-request: a researcher wanted to see which cells drive a significant chi-square, so adjusted standardized residuals were added to the cross-tabs function — jcrosstab(Education ~ Volunteer, data = community, residuals = "adjusted"). That kind of targeted addition is exactly what this phase is for.

title: jstats
section: What to expect at this early stage
text:
What to expect at this early stage
Using jstats during its pre-release phase comes with a simple, honest trade. The costs are small and real: the occasional error message that reads more cryptically than it should, and now and then a feature you wish were there but isn’t yet. The benefit is results fast — and, as the note above says, a direct line to shape what gets built next.
There’s also a safety net that makes the trade an easy one. jstats is a convenience layer, not a cage. Anything it doesn’t do, plain R still does — you can always drop back to base R, or reach for another package, right alongside jstats in the very same script. That’s the intended path: use jstats for the common social-science tasks it’s built for, pick up a little real R painlessly as you go, and when you eventually need something specialized it doesn’t cover, you’ll have learned enough to add the package that does. You’re never stuck, and you’re never learning a dead end.

With jstats installed and loaded, the jstats Quick Start puts it to work.

title: jstats
text:
Once jstats is installed, you can have your Project load it for you every time it opens — so you never type library(jstats) by hand. It’s optional (typing the one line at the start of a session works perfectly well), but worth doing: besides saving the keystrokes, it means the package’s automatic update check runs every time you start work — so you’re always told when a new version is available — and you open each session ready to go. This uses a small setup file called an .Rprofile (a file R reads automatically when it starts).
Create a plain text file named .Rprofile in your Project folder containing these two lines:
# To reinstall jstats (new computer, or after updating R) see the install code at: jma61.github.io/jstats-guides/install-jstats.html
library(jstats)
The second line does the loading. The first is a comment — R ignores any line that starts with # — holding the address of the install page, so the reinstall instructions are one glance away on a new computer or after an R update. Keep the # in place: without it, R would try to run the address as a command at every startup and complain; with it, the line is just a note that costs nothing.
Save it, then restart R (Session menu, Restart R). From now on, every session in this Project starts with jstats already loaded.
Keep this to lightweight startup lines — loading a package, and later a setting or two. Do not put an install command (an install.packages("jstats", ...) line) in .Rprofile: because the file runs on every startup, an install line there tries to reinstall each time and can send RStudio into a loop. Installing is a one-off you run by hand; loading is the repeatable step that belongs in startup.
We’re keeping this minimal for now. Later you’ll add a line or two more — where your data lives, and how many digits to show in output — but this is plenty to start.






NoteGoing deeper (optional)





This .Rprofile lives inside your Project and applies only to this Project. That’s deliberate — it’s self-contained and safe to edit. There’s also a global, all-Projects version, but it’s better left until you’re juggling several Projects at once; the books cover it.

title: jstats
text:
Below are the jstats functions and datasets, grouped by what they do. First, two quick notes on getting help.


Every function has a built-in help page. The quick way to open one in RStudio is to click the function’s name — in your script or the console — and press F1 (or Fn+F1 on most Macs). In any R setup you can also type ? followed by the name — for example, ?jfreq. Either way you get the description, the options, and worked examples.



The function and dataset names below link to those same help pages online — and those pages rebuild themselves automatically from the package. Whenever the package changes or a new function is added, they regenerate from its built-in documentation and the examples are re-run, so the documentation and the code never drift apart: what you see is always current. The payoff is that every example shows the real output the call produces, not output typed in by hand — so you can copy one and trust it matches what you’ll get.






NoteGrowing into a fuller reference



Over time this section will grow into a task-indexed reference — a place to look up how to do a specific thing while you work, organized by what you want to do rather than by function name. For now, it’s a grouped map of what’s available, with each name linking to its full help page.






jdesc — descriptive statistics for one or more variables.
jfreq — frequency tables for categorical variables.
jscreen — a one-call overview of a whole dataset: ranges, missing values, possible outliers, and what kind each variable looks to be.





jt — independent-samples or paired-samples t-test.
jaov — one-way ANOVA.
jcrosstab — cross-tabulation, with an optional chi-square test.
jcorr — a correlation matrix, with p-values and the N behind each pair.
jlm — linear regression.
jlogistic — logistic regression.





jalpha — Cronbach’s alpha reliability analysis for a set of scale items.
jsum — a row-wise sum across several variables (for example, a scale total).
javg — a row-wise mean across several variables (for example, a scale score).





jrecode — recode a variable using an explicit value mapping, with optional labels.
jrelabel — add or change a variable’s variable- and value-labels.
jdeclare_udm — declare user-defined missing values (values that should count as missing, such as a “Refused” code) without overwriting your data.
jsubset — set a case-selection filter so functions run on a chosen subset of cases.
jcomplete — set a listwise complete-case filter so a run of analyses share a matching N.
jcopy — copy a data frame, carrying any of its variable settings along with it.





jload — open a data file (from commercial statistical software, Excel, or CSV) in one command, bringing its value labels, variable labels, and missing-value codes along automatically.
jsave — save a data frame back out to a file.
jconvert — convert missing-value declarations between the conventions different programs use.
jdata_dir — show the folder jstats reads your data files from.





juse — set (or display) the default dataset, so you don’t have to name it in every call.
joptions — set or display session-level options, such as the data folder.
joutput — set how much detail jstats prints (the output verbosity level).
jupdate — update jstats to the latest version (one command; restart R afterward to load it).





jplot — plot a jstats result, or draw a quick plot of variables straight from a dataset.




Most users will not need these. jstats works out what kind each variable is on its own; these let you override that when you want a particular variable handled a particular way.

jnumeric — treat a variable as numeric.
jdummy — treat a categorical variable as a set of dummy variables in regression.
jcount — treat a variable as a count.
jlikert — treat a variable as an ordered (Likert) response item.




jstats ships with two practice datasets, both available the moment the package loads:

community — a clean 100-respondent community survey (income, education, age, a wellbeing score, several yes/no items, a region, and a five-item attitude scale). The package’s default example data; it’s what every function’s help examples use.
clinic — a deliberately messy 70-client mental-health and wellbeing sample, the companion for practicing data cleaning: it carries undeclared missing-value codes, stripped labels, and imperfect scale items to work through.




A few capabilities are planned for future versions — among them regression for count outcomes (Poisson and negative-binomial models) and exporting formatted, APA-style results to Word. The function set above is what ships today.

title: jstats
text: In short, R and RStudio are the free tools you run everything inside, and jstats is an add-on package — the one these guides are about. The two short sections below say what each of these is; if you already work in R, skim them and move on.

title: jstats
text: In short, R and RStudio are the free tools you run everything inside, and jstats is an add-on package — the one these guides are about. The two short sections below say what each of these is; if you already work in R, skim them and move on.

title: jstats
text:
R and RStudio
R is the engine — the free software that does the actual computing and statistics. RStudio is the dashboard you drive it from: a friendlier window onto R, with a place to write your commands, see your results, view your data, and keep your work organized. You install R first, then RStudio on top of it — and from then on you open RStudio, not R. Most people never open R directly at all.

title: jstats
text:
jstats and other packages
R already does a great deal on its own — R as it comes, before you add anything, is usually called base R. But much of R’s power comes from packages: add-ons that teach R new tricks, each a bundle of extra commands that someone has written and shared. Thousands exist, and they tend to fall into a few broad types:

Some take results computed by base R and lay them out in a cleaner, more organized form — often with far less code.
Some import and export data in other programs’ formats — haven, for instance, reads and writes SPSS, Stata, and SAS files.
Some turn base R’s results into graphs and charts — ggplot2 is the best known.
Some add whole analyses base R doesn’t include on its own — for example, mice for filling in missing data, or packages for mixed models and survival analysis.

jstats is one of these add-ons — but rather than specializing in a single one of these jobs, it draws the most useful pieces from across the whole range and bundles them into one more comprehensive package, aimed at the things social scientists do most often. The practical payoff: instead of installing and loading several separate packages just to get going, you can lean on jstats for most of your early work and add other packages as your needs grow. Because jstats stays close to base R rather than inventing its own dialect, those other packages slot in alongside it naturally when the time comes.
Here’s how jstats draws on each type. Most of what it does is type 1: it takes the analyses social scientists use most — descriptives, frequencies, t-tests, ANOVA, regression, correlation, and more — and presents the results in a clean, familiar layout like the one you’d see in commercial statistical software, all from a single short command. It also does type 2 directly, reading and writing labelled SPSS, Stata, and SAS files so they work without a separate import step; a bit of type 3, making basic graphs more quickly and simply than reaching for ggplot2 when you don’t need its full range of options; and the least of type 4 so far, though it adds a few things base R doesn’t include — for example, Gelman (2008) standardized regression coefficients. Throughout, it leans on base R’s own computational engines underneath, so what you learn stays close to base R.

title: jstats
text:
Next steps
One reassurance before you start, because it matters more than it sounds: the everyday analysis commands you’ll be learning here can’t harm your computer, even if you type one wrong. The worst outcome of a mistyped analysis command is a red error message saying R didn’t understand — and errors aren’t a sign you’re doing badly. They’re a normal part of how everyone works in R, beginners and forty-year veterans alike. R can change or delete files, like any program on your computer, but only if you deliberately run file-management commands meant to do that — not the analysis commands these guides cover. The setup steps ahead — installing two programs and a package — are honestly the clumsiest part of the whole journey, and things get easier the moment they’re done. Work through them patiently, lean on the Troubleshooting page if anything snags, and once you’re in, experiment freely.
With this background in mind, you’re ready for Step 2, where we install R and RStudio, and then Step 3, a brief tour of RStudio. Already comfortable in RStudio? Skip ahead to Step 4 (installing jstats) and Step 5 (the Quick Start).
That’s all the background you need to get going. If you’d like a little more first — where the names came from, R’s history, or a closer look at some of R’s best-known packages — the optional boxes below are there to open or skip as you please.






NoteAside — a very short history of R





R’s roots reach back to S, a language created at Bell Labs in the 1970s for statistics and data analysis. (Bell Labs had a taste for one-letter names — it’s the same lab behind the C programming language and the Unix operating system — and S nods to statistics.) S was later turned into commercial software called S-PLUS. Then, in the early 1990s, two statisticians at the University of Auckland in New Zealand — Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman — wrote a free, open-source language of their own in the spirit of S and named it R: the letter just before S, and the shared first initial of both their names. R has been developed ever since by a worldwide volunteer group, the R Core Team, and stays free for everyone — which is a big part of why it has grown so many add-on packages.









NoteAside — where the jstats name comes from





jstats is named on the same principle R itself was. R’s creators — Ross Ihaka and Robert Gentleman, two statisticians in New Zealand — called their language after the first initial they shared. jstats pairs the j of its author’s first name with stats, and every command the package adds begins with that same j — a small signal that lets you tell at a glance which functions come from jstats rather than from base R or another package.









NoteAside — what the tidyverse is





The best-known friendlier layer over R is the tidyverse — a family of packages (dplyr for data wrangling, ggplot2 for graphs, and several more) that share a common style, plus a single meta-package of the same name that installs them together and loads the core ones for you in one step. It became enormously popular by smoothing everyday data work, and its pipe-based style — chaining steps with %>% — shaped how a lot of modern R is written (base R later added its own pipe, |>). jstats shares the goal of smoothing the path but takes a different route, staying close to base R.









NoteAside — the packages jstats builds on





jstats doesn’t reinvent everything from scratch — it stands on several well-regarded packages: ggplot2 for graphs, haven and labelled for reading and handling data (and its value labels) from SPSS, Stata, and SAS, readxl and writexl for Excel files, and rlang for some of the behind-the-scenes machinery. When you install jstats, all of these come along automatically and it uses them under the hood, so you never install or load them yourself. It’s a similar convenience to the way the tidyverse bundles its own family of packages — except jstats keeps these working quietly in the background rather than putting them in your hands to use directly.
You’ll also hear about R Markdown and its newer cousin Quarto — tools for weaving R code and writing together into reports, slides, and websites. These very guides are built with Quarto, with jstats commands and their output sitting right inside the pages you’re reading.









NoteAside — what is CRAN?





When you install R, and most packages, they come from CRAN — the Comprehensive R Archive Network, the official online archive where R itself and thousands of packages are published and kept. It’s maintained by the R community and is free to use. (jstats isn’t on CRAN just yet — for now it installs from another common home for R projects, which the Install jstats page walks you through — but it’s headed there.)
